evidence_spans:

To Whom It May Concern,  

This letter is to formally recognize the remarkable efforts of Karlee Albertson, a 12-year-old sixth-grader from Billings, Montana, who attends Castle Rock Middle School. Over the past six years, Karlee has raised nearly $20,000 to provide anonymous Christmas gifts to struggling families and individuals in her community.  

Karlee’s journey began when she was in first grade and saw a TV commercial about “being the change you wish to see.” After discussing ways she could make a difference with her mother, she decided to start by painting nails at her school’s craft fair and using the proceeds to “adopt” students and families from the school’s Christmas Giving Tree. “I felt it was important to help those less fortunate people who couldn’t afford Christmas,” Karlee explained.  

Her motivation was further inspired when her grandfather, who cherished the holiday season, passed away on Christmas Eve. Karlee resolved to continue his memory by carrying on this meaningful tradition. Since then, she has painted nails at various community events and locations, charging $3 per customer. Her efforts have also been supported by donations of money, gift cards, and nail polish, as well as the growing awareness of her GoFundMe page.  

With the funds she raises, Karlee spends considerable time selecting and wrapping gifts, often with the assistance of a local Boy Scout troop and other volunteers. To date, she has anonymously provided Christmas gifts to more than 250 people.  

Karlee’s vision is one of hope and generosity. “My dream would be that someday, everybody everywhere has a Christmas present under their tree and feels loved and blessed,” she said.  

Karlee Albertson’s compassion, dedication, and unwavering commitment to her community serve as an inspiration to all who learn of her story.  

Sincerely,  
[Your Name]

prompt:
Determine whether the claim is supported by the evidence.
Claim: The writer ran the Madison Mini Marathon at a pace faster than their marathon goal pace.
evidence_spans:
[Your Name]  
[Your Address]  
[City, State, ZIP Code]  
[Email Address]  
[Date]  

Dear [Recipient's Name],  

I hope this letter finds you well. I wanted to share a brief update on my training and a recent race experience.  

This past week was a solid one for running, culminating in the Madison Mini Marathon. I was aiming to run at my marathon goal pace of 9:55 per mile, and I was just under that with a time of 9:42. That said, I don’t believe I could have sustained that pace for much longer that day. I’ve been feeling a bit slow and sluggish lately, though I have to acknowledge that the race was held in hot and hilly conditions. The Fox Cities Marathon, in contrast, is mostly flat, and I’m keeping my fingers crossed for cooler weather on race day.  

Midweek runs were a bit off this week—I lost a little steam—but I did complete a challenging 21-mile run on Saturday. A local running group hosted a “Conquer the Hills” 12-mile event in my town, and I decided to run 8 miles solo before meeting up with them, as the event started at 8 a.m. My 8 miles were solid, and I had to push a bit in the last two to arrive on time, which I did with a few minutes to spare.  

Now, I run hills often and actually enjoy them. I run along the road where this hill challenge was held regularly (I even ran it during my last 20 miler), but I completely underestimated how difficult this run would be. The side roads branching off the main route were brutal. I can’t even begin to describe how steep and long those hills were. It was, by far, the hardest run of my entire life. I was tempted to quit when my hamstrings started cramping, but I walked/hobbled in and refueled with at least two pounds of watermelon and oranges at the finish. I felt completely drained—physically and mentally. The finish line was just one mile from my house, and instead of calling Kevin for a ride, I trudged home and collapsed on my living room floor.  

I will never underestimate a run labeled as a “hill challenge” again. Ever.  

Today, I keep reminding myself that every time my legs feel like jello, it’s great mental training for the marathon. If that didn’t kill me, then five more miles on flat ground in Northeastern Wisconsin won’t, right?  

I hope you are doing well and perhaps training for something exciting as well. Please let me know if you’d like to catch up soon.  

Warm regards,  
[Your Name]

prompt:
Determine whether the claim is supported by the evidence.
Claim: Jermaine Ward, who in 2011 admitted to lying to police to avoid arrest for driving while disqualified, now claims he was forced at gunpoint by Dale Cregan to act as the getaway driver in the murder of David Short.
evidence_spans:
Dale Cregan’s alleged getaway driver in a brutal gun-and-grenade murder case has a long criminal history, jurors were told in a tense courtroom at Preston Crown Court. Jermaine Ward, 24, is currently serving a 32-month prison sentence for producing amphetamine, a fact that emerged as the trial entered its 46th day.

Ward had been due to appear at Bolton Crown Court on August 10, 2022, in connection with a separate charge—just one day before the Crown alleges he was involved in the murder of David Short, 46, alongside Cregan and Anthony Wilkinson. Instead, Ward appeared in court on October 1 of that same year and pleaded guilty to producing amphetamine. That day, he was sentenced to 32 months in prison and has been serving it ever since.

Now, standing in the witness box, Ward denied playing any active role in the murder of David Short, claiming he was forced at gunpoint by Cregan. He also denied charges of attempted murder and causing an explosion with a hand grenade. The court heard about Ward’s extensive criminal record, including drug possession, property damage, perverting the course of justice, and driving while disqualified.

In 2011, Ward had been stopped by police on the M6 toll road while driving a BMW X5 despite being banned from driving. He gave a false name and produced a fake driving licence, but his real identity was uncovered after he was fingerprinted. “I just didn’t want to be arrested for banned driving,” Ward admitted to the court. He was later jailed for perverting the course of justice and driving while disqualified.

Ward told the court he had been staying with a friend named Lee Kelly, who asked him to drive to London to “sort a problem out” with the mother of one of Kelly’s children. “He told me it was important and he really needed me to help him,” Ward said, later admitting he had been “wrong” to get behind the wheel and assist.

The trial took an unexpected turn when one of the jurors reported feeling unwell and was taken to hospital during the morning session. The judge, Mr Justice Holroyde, informed the remaining jurors that their colleague had been seen by paramedics and was being checked for a head injury sustained in a recent accident. “We hope it doesn’t turn out to be anything serious,” he said, before adjourning the trial until 10am the following day.

Dale Cregan, 29, continues to deny two counts of murder, four counts of attempted murder, and causing an explosion with a hand grenade. However, he has admitted to murdering two police officers. The trial remains ongoing.

prompt:

Claim: Japan's National Institute of Fruit Science discovered in a 2021 study that the Fuji apple variety contains a specific allele of the polyphenol oxidase gene which decreases enzymatic browning by 40% relative to other common apple cultivars.
evidence_spans:
**PCS Art Teacher Shares Creative Spring Break Activities: Rock Stacking, Apple Carving, and Tape Resist Art**

PCS K-6 Visual Art Teacher Jonathan Graffius recently shared a collection of creative and calming activities he and his daughter enjoyed over spring break. From rock stacking to apple carving and a fun art technique called "Tape Resist," the projects highlight the joy of connecting with nature, experimenting with art, and embracing the process of creation.

Rock stacking, an ancient practice known as "Cairns," was a favorite for Graffius. Originally used as markers or memorials, rock stacking today is a meditative and symbolic activity. "I think it’s great to get outside, connect with nature, battle gravity, and creatively stack rocks," Graffius said. "I came away feeling calm and balanced—just like the rocks. Be optimistic and persevere through the challenges."

Another standout project was an apple carving experiment. Graffius chose a Fuji apple with a few blemishes and used a vegetable peeler to remove the skin. He then carefully planned the facial features—eyes, ears, nose, mouth, and lips—considering which would protrude and which would recede. Using a light pencil sketch, he carved the apple, removing sections to create depth and form. "The hardest part is not cutting off too much of the protruding features," he noted. "If you make some mistakes, don’t stress too much. Once the apple dries and shrivels, it often looks great. And if not, you can always eat the scraps and start over."

Graffius shared that his daughter enjoyed eating the carved-out apple chunks, and he set the finished piece on a plate to dry. As the apple dries, it becomes browner, more wrinkled, and harder, adding character to the final piece.

For those looking to try a new art technique, Graffius encouraged using "Tape Resist." This method involves placing tape on a surface before painting or drawing. The tape blocks the paint or drawing from reaching the surface, creating clean, defined edges when the tape is removed. Graffius shared a photo of his daughter using packing tape to create a resist pattern, then coloring in the shapes with chalk. "It’s a versatile technique that can be used with many media and done indoors or outdoors," he said. "Have fun and be creative."

Jonathan Graffius is the K-6 Visual Art teacher at PCS, and his personal art includes paintings, oil pastels, photography, and sculpture. The purpose of his blog is to keep the PCS community informed of current art news and activities, while also encouraging student creativity and artistic expression. Members of the PCS community are invited to participate by adding comments, and the blog is maintained to ensure a positive and supportive environment. For any questions or concerns, contact Graffius directly at: firstname.lastname@example.com.

evidence_spans:
**A Day in the Life of Water Damage Restoration: How ServiceMaster Restorations Helped a Home in Lehigh Acres**

Water damage can strike at any time, even when you least expect it. That’s exactly what happened in Lehigh Acres, Florida, where a burst pipe in the kitchen led to significant water damage throughout a home—impacting the kitchen, living room, foyer, and garage. But when the unexpected happens, you need a reliable team on your side. That’s where ServiceMaster Restorations stepped in.

After hours, when the situation was already worsening, ServiceMaster was called to the scene. Their first step was to extract the standing water to prevent further damage. Once the water was out of the way, the drying process began in earnest. Technicians strategically placed dehumidifiers and air movers throughout the home to speed up the drying and reduce the risk of mold.

One of the most critical steps in the restoration process was the removal of baseboards. This allowed the drywall to dry completely and ensured that no hidden moisture would lead to mold growth behind the walls. Unfortunately, the water had also damaged the laminate flooring, which had to be removed along with the affected baseboards and drywall.

To take an extra precaution, ServiceMaster applied an anti-microbial agent to all affected areas. This proactive step helps prevent mold from taking hold and ensures the home remains safe for its occupants.

The restoration is still underway, with ServiceMaster working closely with the homeowner to restore the property to its pre-damage condition. Once the water damage is fully remediated, ServiceMaster’s skilled construction team will begin the rebuilding phase. This includes rehanging drywall, painting walls, and installing new laminate flooring—essentially returning the home to its original, beautiful state.

From the initial call to the long-term restoration, the ServiceMaster team has shown dedication, professionalism, and a deep understanding of what it takes to recover from water damage. As one satisfied project participant shared: *“I thought our meeting with Jonathan, Kim, Diane and you regarding our project was very productive and I believe that the resulting meeting of minds suggests that repair and remodeling should proceed successfully.”*

In times of crisis, it’s reassuring to know that trusted professionals like ServiceMaster Restorations are just a call away.

prompt:
Determine whether the claim is supported by the evidence.
Claim: The genealogical record of Confucius's descendants was inscribed on UNESCO's Memory of the World Register in 2018.
evidence_spans:
By Bill Smith, dpa  
QUFU, China—An epitaph in the historic town of Qufu states, “Comrade Kong Xiangguo tragically lost his life in the line of duty and gave his life for the nation.”  

Kong, who was 25 years old, was among the 70 crew members who perished aboard the People's Liberation Navy's No. 361 submarine during a training exercise off the coast of northeastern China in 2003.  

According to his gravestone in the Confucius Forest—a scenic burial park and one of Qufu’s three main tourist attractions—Kong was a Communist Party member and a “75th-generation descendant of Confucius” born in Qufu.  

Confucius, known in China as Kong Zi, is traditionally believed to have been born near Qufu in 551 BC and died in 479 BC. Like Socrates in ancient Greece, Confucius is a semi-legendary figure in Chinese history, with most accepted accounts of his life coming from writings composed after his death.  

Many of his followers helped spread Confucian teachings, and later scholars adapted them to fit evolving social and political contexts or their own philosophical views.  

Approximately two million people worldwide today bear the surname Kong, though most of their ancestors adopted the name long after Confucius’s time.  

Over the past two and a half millennia, Confucius has experienced both high and low points in popularity. The Han Dynasty (206 B.C. — A.D. 220) elevated him to near-divine status, and Qufu became a major pilgrimage site, comparable to Mecca. For centuries, Confucius Temples have stood in most major Chinese cities.  

In the early 20th century, the Nationalist government used Confucianism to promote obedience, and the sage’s central role in Chinese moral philosophy seemed secure until Mao Zedong, China’s first Communist leader, attacked him during the Cultural Revolution (1966–1976). During this period, statues of Confucius were destroyed in Qufu, and graves in the Confucius Forest were defiled.  

Kong Lingshao, a teenager during the Cultural Revolution, recalled to state media in January, “As a descendant of Confucius, I can’t find the words to describe the humiliation I felt.”  

Mao positioned himself as a potential philosopher-king and sought to eliminate both Confucius and a key political rival in the “Criticize Lin Biao and Confucius” campaign.  

With the resurgence of nationalism and patriotism, the Communist Party has embraced Confucianism as part of a broader revival of traditional Chinese culture. The “spiritual civilization” campaign, launched in the mid-1990s in response to concerns about growing materialism, promoted both Maoist and Confucian values.  

The party appears to be relying on this new ideological approach to extend its rule over China under the banner of stability, which the government uses to justify its continued suppression of dissent.

In the early 20th century, the Nationalist government used Confucianism to promote obedience, and the sage’s central role in Chinese moral philosophy seemed secure until Mao Zedong, China’s first Communist leader, attacked him during the Cultural Revolution (1966–1976). During this period, statues of Confucius were destroyed in Qufu, and graves in the Confucius Forest were defiled.  

Kong Lingshao, a teenager during the Cultural Revolution, recalled to state media in January, “As a descendant of Confucius, I can’t find the words to describe the humiliation I felt.”  

Mao positioned himself as a potential philosopher-king and sought to eliminate both Confucius and a key political rival in the “Criticize Lin Biao and Confucius” campaign.  

With the resurgence of nationalism and patriotism, the Communist Party has embraced Confucianism as part of a broader revival of traditional Chinese culture. The “spiritual civilization” campaign, launched in the mid-1990s in response to concerns about growing materialism, promoted both Maoist and Confucian values.  

The party appears to be relying on this new ideological approach to extend its rule over China under the banner of stability, which the government uses to justify its continued suppression of dissent.

evidence_spans:
**Team Meeting Dialogue:**

**Kari:** Okay, so I was in the lobby on Sunday morning, and I overheard someone asking a pretty urgent question about Summit’s new musical. You know, the one with the audition posters up around the church?

**O.J.:** Oh, you mean *The Road to Zamboria*? Yeah, that’s the one. Based on the Good Samaritan parable. It’s from the same team that did *The Prodigal Musical* last year, which was pretty well-received.

**Lena:** Right, and that was the first time we did a musical at the Fringe Festival. For those who missed it, the Fringe is this two-week event with all kinds of fringe theatre—uncensored, unjuried, and pretty much anything goes.

**Kari:** Exactly. And I think that “anything-goes” vibe is what made that Sunday question so confusing. Why would a church be prepping to perform at a festival that’s so… non-traditional?

**Lena:** That’s a powerful story. It shows how stepping outside our walls can impact others. But now I’m wondering—what about the impact on *us*? What does it mean for the church to be at the Fringe?

**Kari:** That’s exactly what I was thinking. Instead of asking what the Fringe was doing at the church, we should be asking what the church is doing at the Fringe. And who better to ask than the people behind the musical?

**O.J.:** True. We need to hear from the co-director and co-writer. I think we’re only halfway through this story.

**Lena:** Yeah, this feels like the first half of a blog post. But don’t worry—next week, I’ll be interviewing Darling Heldt and Michael Murray. In the meantime, we should make sure everyone knows where to find the show.

prompt:
Determine whether the claim is supported by the evidence.
Claim: Leeds won the Challenge Cup Final this season.
evidence_spans:
Leeds' coach Brian McDermott has been highlighted by chief executive Gary Hetherington as crucial to the team's Grand Final qualification. Leeds secured their sixth Super League final in eight years, and their fourth in five against St Helens, with a 26-24 victory over Warrington on Friday.

“We definitely wouldn’t be in the Grand Final without his contributions,” Hetherington said on BBC Radio Leeds. “Hiring Brian McDermott has been one of the best decisions I've made in my 15 years at the club,” he added. “I’ve had some good and not-so-good choices, but bringing in Brian during a challenging period in the club’s evolution has proven to be the right one.”

McDermott, who previously won the Grand Final with Brian Noble’s Bradford in 2001, has now guided Leeds to their second final of the season—just five weeks after their Challenge Cup Final appearance at Wembley, where they lost 28-18 to Wigan. After finding their rhythm late in the season, particularly with a strong second-half performance at Wembley, Leeds finished fifth in the Super League. Hetherington praised the Rhinos for becoming the first team outside the top four to reach the Grand Final, defeating Warrington—who had won the League Leaders' Shield.

“For everyone at the stadium and watching at home, this has to be our finest hour,” Hetherington remarked. “It puts us into another Grand Final, and now it's all about winning it next week. It was a remarkable game of rugby league—sport at its best. The intensity and pace from start to finish were incredible, and both teams gave their all.

“When we were under pressure, six points down, and facing a strong Warrington side, the courage, spirit, and determination of the Rhinos were outstanding.”

Hetherington hopes the whole of Leeds will support the Rhinos and travel to Old Trafford next Saturday to celebrate another memorable day for the city.

Leeds have a solid record in the Grand Final, having won four of their six previous appearances at Old Trafford. After losing their first in 1998, 10-4 to Wigan, they faced Brian Noble’s Bradford in 2004 and 2005. Leeds won 16-8 under Tony Smith in 2004, only to lose 15-6 a year later when Noble’s Bulls avenged their defeat—marking the last time an English coach won the trophy. Since then, Leeds have been victorious in all three of their Grand Final appearances, defeating St Helens 33-6 in 2007 in Smith’s final game as coach, followed by two tighter wins under Brian McClennan in 2008 and 2009.

prompt:
Determine whether the claim is supported by the evidence.
Claim: Existing-home sales were set to rise by 7 percent.
evidence_spans:
In the wake of a housing crisis that had kept first-time home buyers on the sidelines, a new chapter was beginning to unfold. As 2015 approached, realtor.com® predicted that these young, eager buyers would finally return to the market, setting the stage for a fresh wave of home ownership.

Jonathan Smoke, chief economist for realtor.com®, explained that the lingering financial effects of the recession had kept Millennials from stepping into the housing market. But with employment opportunities on the rise, he believed this would change. “As younger buyers gain more financial stability, they’ll feel empowered to make the leap,” he said. “If credit access improves, we could see a significant increase in their numbers—especially in more affordable regions like the Midwest and South.”

The story of 2015 would be written by Millennials, who were expected to form a large portion of new households. With the economy gaining strength and the forecast of 2.5 million new jobs, young adults were beginning to build lives of their own—often starting with a home. These two factors—job growth and household formation—were key to reinvigorating the housing market.

As more first-time buyers entered, existing-home sales were set to rise by 8 percent. Unlike in 2012, when distressed properties had driven much of the growth, this time the increase would come from a healthier, more balanced market. The days of relying heavily on foreclosed or short-sale homes were fading.

Home prices, too, were expected to climb. Realtor.com® forecasters predicted a 4.5 percent increase for the year. While this was good news for sellers, it posed a challenge for first-time buyers, especially in high-cost areas like San Francisco and San Jose. These young buyers were expected to gravitate toward more affordable markets with strong job markets—places like Des Moines, Atlanta, and Houston.

Mortgage rates, currently at a low of 3.89 percent, were also on the rise. By mid-2015, the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage was expected to climb to around 5 percent as the Federal Reserve raised interest rates. While the 1-year adjustable-rate mortgage would rise more slowly, the overall increase in rates would affect affordability for first-timers. Many would be pushed toward less expensive regions, where the dream of homeownership was still within reach.

Despite the rising prices and mortgage rates, housing affordability was not expected to fall too far. While realtor.com® predicted a 5 to 10 percent decline in affordability, this would likely be balanced by rising wages. When viewed through the lens of history, the housing market for 2015 still looked strong.

It was a year of cautious optimism. For many first-time buyers, the path to homeownership was still steep, but the destination was finally in sight.

prompt:
Determine whether the claim is supported by the evidence.
Claim: Smartphone users' awareness of the distinction between Apple iOS and Android systems indicates that competitor use of 'App Store' is unlikely to cause customer confusion.
evidence_spans:
**Formal Report: Legal Dispute Over the Trademark of "App Store"**

Apple Inc. has long maintained that the term “App Store” is a registered trademark and has pursued legal action to prevent other companies from using the term in the names of their online app stores. This effort has targeted competing services such as Google’s Android Play app store and BlackBerry’s app store, with Apple asserting that the use of the term by multiple companies causes confusion among its customers.

In 2011, Apple took legal action against Amazon for using the term “Appstore” in the name of its Android-based app store. However, recent developments have cast doubt on the strength of Apple’s legal position. U.S. District Judge Phyllis Hamilton has expressed skepticism regarding Apple’s claim that the use of the term by competitors leads to customer confusion. In a recent statement, Judge Hamilton noted that smartphone users are generally aware of the distinction between the Apple iOS and Android systems. She questioned the basis of Apple’s argument, stating, “Where’s the confusion? There’s some suggestion [by Apple] that if Amazon is using the ‘Appstore’ term someone might think they have as many apps as Apple does. Well, why? And how, in fact, does that contribute to any deception on the part of Amazon?”

Industry experts have expressed surprise that Apple has not yet withdrawn its legal challenge. Apple first filed for a trademark on the term “App Store” in 2008. Although the application was initially rejected, it was later approved in 2010. Microsoft, however, filed an objection in July 2010, arguing that the term was too generic to be trademarked. Microsoft also requested the Trademark Trial and Appeal Board to deny Apple’s application, and the legal dispute between the two companies continues, with both sides filing counterclaims.

To avoid legal repercussions, several technology companies have opted for alternative names for their app stores. For example, HP uses the term “App Catalog,” BlackBerry uses “App World,” and Google has branded its store as the “Android Market.”

The term “App Store” appears to be widely used and understood in the industry, raising questions about its eligibility for exclusive trademark protection. Some have likened the situation to attempting to trademark a generic phrase such as “operating system.” The ongoing legal battle highlights the broader debate over the balance between brand protection and the use of common terminology in the technology sector.

Claim: According to exclusive information from LIB, Peter has sent a termination letter to the group’s lawyer, Festus Keyamo (SAN), formally requesting the dissolution of the agreement between the members of Psquare.
evidence_spans:
**Interviewer:** Thank you for joining us today. To start off, can you confirm the recent reports that the Nigerian music group Psquare has officially split up?

**Expert:** Yes, I can confirm that. According to exclusive information from LIB, Peter has sent a termination letter to the group’s lawyer, Festus Keyamo (SAN), formally requesting the dissolution of the agreement between the members of Psquare.

**Interviewer:** What are the reasons Peter is giving for this split?

**Expert:** In the termination letter—which has been obtained by LIB—Peter outlines several serious allegations against his twin brother, Paul, and their older brother and group manager, Jude. He claims that Paul has become uncooperative and even canceled their planned US tour this year without informing him.

**Interviewer:** That sounds like a major breakdown in communication. Are there other accusations?

**Expert:** Yes. Peter also accuses Paul of slandering his wife and children on social media with what he describes as “lies.” As a result, he says they’ve been receiving threatening messages.

**Interviewer:** And what about Jude? What role does he play in this?

**Expert:** Peter alleges that Jude once threatened to kill him and shoot his wife, Lola. This reportedly happened in the presence of Paul and former First Lady of Cross River State, Mrs. Imoke. Peter also claims that Jude threatened to bring a coffin for him if he ever came near his house.

**Interviewer:** These are very serious allegations. Has there been any response from Paul or Jude?

**Expert:** Not publicly at this time. The situation remains tense, and it appears Peter has exhausted all options to reconcile with his brothers before making the decision to part ways legally.

**Interviewer:** How significant is this split for the Nigerian music industry?

**Expert:** It’s a major development. Psquare has been a dominant force in Nigerian music for years, and their split is not only a shock to their fans but also a sign of deeper personal and professional conflicts.

**Mayor Pro Tem Caraway Dominates Public Safety Committee Meeting with Gun, Youth, and Tobacco Talk**

Mayor Pro Tem Dwaine Caraway took center stage at the latest Public Safety Committee meeting held at 1500 Marilla, delivering a passionate and extended commentary on gun violence, youth crime, and 24-hour tobacco shops—far removed from the routine crime statistics that typically define such gatherings.

The meeting began with outgoing Detroit Police Department Chief David Kunkle presenting year-to-date crime statistics. Following that, Caraway seized the opportunity to pivot the discussion toward gun control, declaring, “I’m just on a gun tear this morning,” and acknowledging his own history with two previous gun buy-back initiatives.

Caraway pushed for a deeper conversation on the “illegal acquisition of handguns,” naming pawn shops, gun shows, and the black market as key sources. However, Kunkle countered that many of the weapons used in crimes are obtained legally, often through flea markets and gun shows, which he described as more problematic than pawn shops. Kunkle emphasized that pawn shops are highly regulated and subject to detailed background checks.

The discussion then shifted to concerns about rising juvenile crime during summer break. Committee members Tennell Atkins and Caraway both raised the issue of how to prevent an uptick in youth-related offenses during the warmer months. Kunkle and other officers suggested community-based solutions like midnight basketball leagues and other outreach programs to keep teens engaged and off the streets.

Caraway, however, was skeptical. “The thugs aren’t gonna come to midnight basketball,” he said, urging the city to develop more direct and targeted programs to address the issue. He railed against the presence of “thugs” who, he claimed, were taking over public spaces like recreation centers and parks.

The conversation naturally led to Caraway’s ongoing concerns about 24-hour tobacco shops, which he said were proliferating in his district. “We know they’re selling more than just tobacco products,” he said, calling for stricter oversight and even suggesting the city adopt measures similar to those in New York, including the installation of surveillance cameras.

Caraway’s remarks, as always, were as much a sermon as they were a policy discussion, blending frustration with a call to action. “If it takes cameras, we’re gonna camera-up the city,” he concluded, reinforcing his push for more aggressive tactics in the fight against crime.

Claim: 9.1% of small businesses along seven major shopping strips are either closed or closing, a rate significantly higher than the national average of 4% to 7%.
evidence_spans:
If you're looking for a thriving small business community in the Bronx, you might be disappointed. A recent report by Rep. Anthony Weiner reveals that 9.1% of small businesses along seven major shopping strips are either closed or closing, a rate significantly higher than the national average of 4% to 7%. The situation is dire, with "For Rent" and "Going Out of Business" signs becoming all too common. 

Local business owners like Michael Palomba, who has run a music and dance academy for over 50 years, are struggling as the area deteriorates. He points to rising taxes, frequent citations, and the growing dominance of chain stores and the internet as major contributors to the decline. Palomba, who survived a tragic robbery at his store, says the city's policies are "choking" small businesses.

While the federal government has offered some relief through the Small Business Administration with $730 million in loans and reduced fees, many like Waley Alzubide, owner of a bodega on East Gun Hill Road, remain skeptical. With rising costs and shrinking customer spending, it's hard to see how these measures will be enough to revive the American dream for local entrepreneurs. The Bronx is losing its small business soul, and it's unclear if anything short of major systemic change will turn the tide.

evidence_spans:

I am writing to provide a detailed account of the events that transpired on April 2009 in Pasco County, Florida, involving John Croft and his neighbor, Seth Sigmon. Mr. Croft, who now walks with a cane and has experienced difficulty speaking due to a stroke, testified in court regarding the incident.

According to Mr. Croft, the confrontation began after he asked Seth Sigmon’s 9-year-old son to stop playing on his dumpster. Mr. Croft admitted that he may have been somewhat rude in his tone. He explained that this seemingly minor interaction led to a significant escalation in the relationship between the two neighbors.

Mr. Croft stated that Seth Sigmon became angry and confronted him, allegedly pushing him in the stomach and using offensive language. Mr. Croft, who was 81 years old at the time, claimed that Sigmon threatened to throw him into the dumpster. In response, Mr. Croft retrieved his .22 caliber pistol and warned Sigmon not to approach him.

Mr. Croft testified that when Sigmon advanced toward him, he fired a single shot to the chest. He denied that any further shots were fired after Sigmon had fallen to the ground. However, prosecutors have alleged that two additional shots were fired, striking Sigmon in the back while he was on the ground and motionless. They also claim that Sigmon never physically threatened or touched Mr. Croft.

Mr. Croft emphasized that prior to the incident, he had a good relationship with Sigmon and his son, even purchasing toys for the child in the past.

Closing arguments in the case are scheduled to begin on Thursday afternoon. Mr. Croft faces a potential 30-year sentence if convicted.

Sincerely,  
[Your Name]

Claim: The Good Night Lights campaign started by Steve Brosnihan at Hasbro Children's Hospital now includes the Rhode Island State House dome.
evidence_spans:
**Interviewer:** Governor Raimondo, thank you for joining us. You recently announced that the State House dome will be participating in the Good Night Lights campaign. Can you tell us more about this initiative?

**Governor Gina M. Raimondo:** Absolutely. Starting this evening, the dome of the State House will flash for 30 seconds every Wednesday at 8:30 p.m. as part of the Good Night Lights campaign. It’s a beautiful way to spread joy to sick children at Hasbro Children's Hospital and to show the kind of community spirit that Rhode Island is known for.

**Interviewer:** What inspired you to get the State House involved?

**Governor Raimondo:** One of the things I love most about Rhode Island is our incredible sense of community. Good Night Lights is a perfect example of that. Being able to give a sick child a few moments of joy is a wonderful gift. I hope the State House participating in this will encourage others to get involved as well.

**Interviewer:** Can you tell us the story behind Good Night Lights?

**Governor Raimondo:** The campaign was started by Steve Brosnihan, a cartoonist from Bristol who spent years volunteering with sick children by teaching them to draw. In 2010, he told a young patient at Hasbro Children's Hospital to look out the window for his blinking flashlight. That small gesture grew into a movement that has expanded to other states and hospitals in recent years.

**Interviewer:** What impact do you think the State House’s participation will have?

**Governor Raimondo:** Steve Brosnihan, the Conductor of Good Night Lights, said that the participation of the State House dome will add a major, exciting element to the display on Wednesday nights. I believe it will bring joy to patients, families, and staff at Hasbro, and it will also symbolize the caring heart and determined hope of all Rhode Islanders for our hospitalized children.

**Interviewer:** What would you say to Rhode Islanders who are interested in getting involved?

**Governor Raimondo:** I would encourage them to visit Hasbro Children's Hospital’s website to learn more about Good Night Lights and find ways to participate. Every little bit helps, and together, we can make a big difference in the lives of these children.

Claim: The Liberty ship SS John W. Brown was repurposed as a floating barracks for veterans in New York Harbor during the 1946 housing crisis.
evidence_spans:
Set in the shadow of post-World War II New York, where housing shortages and unemployment loomed like a heavy winter fog, the story unfolds in a grand, empty mansion on 5th Avenue. Each winter, the mansion sits untouched, its owner fleeing to sunnier climes, leaving behind a palace of marble and mystery. It’s here that Aloysious McKeever—better known as Mac—makes his home.

Mac is a man of contradictions: a homeless man with a heart of gold, a man who breaks into the mansion not for malice, but for necessity. With only his loyal dog for company, he lives off the mansion’s lavish pantry and sleeps in its plush closets, moving on when the time is right. But this winter, things are different.

As the snow begins to fall, Mac finds himself joined by others—unemployed veterans with young children in tow, a widow with a past she won’t share, and even the mansion’s wealthy owner, who has returned under a false name. Each has their own reason for being there, some noble, some less so. Mac, ever the reluctant leader, takes it upon himself to keep order, offering advice on hard work, respect, and the value of human connection—though he himself is a bachelor who prefers solitude.

Despite his rough edges, Mac is a man of charm and quiet wisdom. He lounges in a smoking jacket, puffing on a cigar, and yet his words carry weight. He teaches by example, even as he breaks the rules. And as the weeks pass, the unlikely household begins to form bonds—of friendship, of trust, and of redemption.

Through laughter and hardship, the mansion becomes more than just a shelter. It becomes a place where people from all walks of life learn to see each other not by their circumstances, but by their hearts. And in the end, it’s not just about who owns the mansion, but who truly belongs in it.

It’s a story about how much is too much, about the responsibilities of the wealthy, and the power of not judging a book by its cover. And at its heart is Mac—a man who, despite everything, finds a way to make a home out of nothing at all.

Claim: Blockchain continues to capture the imagination of banks and technologists alike.
evidence_spans:
**Exciting Innovations to Watch at This Year’s Insight and Innovation Day**

As we wrap up our preview series for this year’s Insight and Innovation Day in Boston on April 4th, we’re thrilled to shine a spotlight on one of the most exciting additions to our Model Bank Awards program: the **Emerging Innovation** category.

When we introduced this category, we weren’t sure what we’d find. But we were *definitely* not disappointed. The nominations were impressive in both number and scope, showcasing how banks are pushing the boundaries of what’s possible in the financial world. From early-stage experiments to full-blown innovations, these stories are not just interesting—they’re pointing the way toward the **future of banking**.

Let’s take a closer look at some of the most compelling trends we saw:

---

**1. Innovative Customer Engagement**

The most forward-thinking banks are meeting their customers where they are—on social media, on their wrists, and in their everyday lives. We saw exciting examples of banks using **chatbots** and **wearables** to create seamless, real-time interactions. Some are even exploring how to bring **virtual agents** into physical branches to enhance in-person service.

These tools aren’t just for show—they’re being used to:

- Power customer service through **written or verbal conversations** via chatbots and phone.
- Support **human agents** with AI-driven insights.
- Help banks **personalize marketing** and offer the best possible deal to each customer.

---

**2. Biometrics: Beyond Fingerprints**

Security and convenience have never been more important—and banks are stepping up their game with **biometric authentication**. While fingerprint recognition is still common, we’re seeing a big shift toward **facial** and **voice recognition**. 

This isn’t just for individual customers. **Corporate banking** is also starting to embrace biometrics, opening up new possibilities for secure, fast, and frictionless transactions.

---

**3. APIs: The New Banking Infrastructure**

We’ve talked before about **Open Banking**, but APIs are so much more than a regulatory requirement. Leading banks around the world are using APIs to become **platforms for innovation**.

Some are opening up their services to third-party developers, while others are building **marketplace banking models**—positioning themselves as the central hub for a wide range of financial services. This is where the future of banking is being built.

---

**4. Blockchain: From Experiment to Enterprise**

Blockchain continues to capture the imagination of banks and technologists alike. We were excited to see so many nominations in this space, from **collaborative cross-border payment solutions** to **blockchain-based employee incentive programs**.

Some banks are working together on **trade finance** and **settlement systems**, while others are using blockchain to streamline internal processes. It’s clear that this technology is moving from the experimental phase to real-world application.

---

**Join Us in Boston on April 4th!**

We’ll be diving deep into all of these topics and more at our **Insight and Innovation Day** next week. It’s also the moment we announce the **Model Bank winners**, including our prestigious **Model Bank of the Year**.

We’re in the final stretch of preparations and are more excited than ever. The event has been **sold out for weeks**, so if you haven’t registered yet, it’s likely too late. If you *are* coming, we can’t wait to see you in Boston. And if your plans have changed, please let us know—there are others on the waiting list who would love a chance to attend.

See you on April 4th!

prompt:
Determine whether the claim is supported by the evidence.
Claim: When testing paint adhesion on polystyrene miniature models, a 35% isopropyl alcohol solution is required to prevent micro-fracturing of the substrate while maintaining test validity, as higher concentrations disrupt the polymer matrix at a molecular level.
evidence_spans:
For a miniature painting project, the right kind of paint is essential. Before heading to a miniature painting service in the UK, it’s a good idea to check the paint currently on your walls. Determining whether it’s latex or oil-based is a simple process. Start by washing a small section of the wall with warm water and a bit of detergent. Once the area is dry, take a cotton ball or small piece of cotton and dip it in an alcohol solution. Gently rub it across the cleaned surface.

If the paint begins to come off, it’s likely latex. In that case, a new layer of latex paint will probably be needed for the project. It’s best to stick with the same type to avoid complications. However, if the paint doesn’t budge during the test, it’s likely oil-based. That means an oil-based paint will need to be used on that area. Before applying the new paint, a primer should be applied to the surface. This step is important to prevent a latex paint from being used on top of oil paint, which could lead to peeling.

There’s also a final test to consider—especially if the miniature was built before 1977 and hasn’t had a fresh coat of paint since. In such cases, it’s wise to check for lead paint. A lead paint testing kit can help determine if lead is present in the existing paint. However, the results from these kits aren’t always accurate, so it might be best to consult a professional for a more reliable assessment.

Claim: This week, we have Jean Yuho Ford coming in to serve as the head trainee for the fall.
evidence_spans:
**Team Meeting Dialogue:**

**Mira:**  
One of the things you notice if you live at Yokoji for a while is just how many people come and go through the temple gates.

**Tomas:**  
Yeah, we have visitors all the time—day trippers, retreat participants, hikers heading to the Pacific Crest Trail, delivery trucks... the list goes on.

**Lena:**  
Exactly. But beyond all the temporary visitors, even the more permanent faces tend to be, well, impermanent. Today, we say goodbye to Alex.

**Mira:**  
Alex has been living at Yokoji over the summer and has been training with us on and off for a few years before her residency. She’s leaving to pursue an MA in design at the Royal College of Art in London.

**Tomas:**  
It’s always bittersweet to see residents go. But it’s also encouraging. Alex knew she’d be leaving at the end of summer and used this time to engage in Zen practice before moving on to the next phase of her life.

**Lena:**  
People have different paths. For some, Yokoji is a stepping stone—a chance to clarify their direction before moving forward.

**Mira:**  
I’m glad our training center is flexible enough to allow training for anyone who comes in, regardless of how long they stay. The training here provides a container, but it's up to each trainee to bring it to life—there are no time constraints.

**Tomas:**  
As Tenshin Roshi often says, you have to live and enjoy your life—no one else is going to do it for you.

**Lena:**  
And while people are leaving, others are arriving. This week, we have Jean Yuho Ford coming in to serve as the head trainee for the fall.

**Mira:**  
We also have another trainee from Utah arriving via the Palm Springs bus station on Saturday.

**Tomas:**  
Next week, Kate Wilson returns. She trained with us for six months but had to go back to Canada when her tourist visa expired.

**Lena:**  
We applied for and successfully received a religious worker’s visa for her. She can now train here for another 36 months, with the possibility of extending it to five years.

**Mira:**  
We also have a few other familiar faces returning this fall. Susan Tipton, who’s featured on the zazen page and video, will be here for the beginning of the training period.

**Tomas:**  
James Halsey, Thomas Cohn from Sweden—who trained with us a few years ago—and a number of others, including Lex Hanu Latkovski and possibly Bryan Kokai Mestman, will be arriving later in the fall.

**Lena:**  
That’s the beauty of Yokoji—the revolving door, the gateless gate. There’s constant coming and going, but at the core, it always remains the same.

**Mira:**  
With the training period only weeks away, we hope to see many of you here for the sesshins and regular schedule over the next few months.

**Tomas:**  
Join us for a day, or for the whole period—you’re always welcome to be part of this ever-changing community.

The app, priced at $5.99, offers peace of mind to frequent buyers of used phones, potentially saving them from purchasing unusable devices. A cheaper, 99-cent-per-use version is also in development for occasional users.

prompt:

Claim: Alice and the other children mutually frustrate each other.
evidence_spans:
Alice had no idea why rabbits and chocolate eggs went together. The whole idea of hunting for eggs seriously confused her. After stuffing a third egg into her dress pocket, she decided she’d rather take a nap. So, she lay under a big shady oak tree, away from the other kids. Not that she didn’t like them—well, maybe she didn’t like them *that* much. They just never seemed to question anything.  

Why is the sun blue?  
Why can’t a horse talk?  
Why is it a bunny and not a cat?  

Because. Because. Because.  

They got tired of her questions. She got tired of their answers. Eventually, she fell asleep, thinking to herself, *How clever I am. This is what Easter should be—rest, not a boring egg hunt.* 🐰🥚💤

Claim: Mattel Films was launched in August.
evidence_spans:
**Team Meeting Dialogue:**

**Alex (Marketing Lead):**  
Alright, let's start with the latest from the CEO. Kreiz is still confident in the toy business, even after some recent challenges. He mentioned that Q4 sales were down, but it's mostly because of the Toys R Us closure.  

**Jamie (Finance Lead):**  
Right, and he pointed out that Barbie and Hot Wheels actually did better than expected. That’s a positive sign. It shows that the core brands are still holding strong.  

**Raj (Product Development):**  
Kreiz has been at the helm since April 2018, and he’s the fourth CEO in five years. He came in with a clear plan to revive the company by expanding into movies, TV, and video games. That’s a big shift for Mattel.  

**Lena (Strategic Planning):**  
Exactly. They even launched their own movie studio, Mattel Films, in September. That’s part of the push to turn their iconic toys into multimedia content.  

**Alex:**  
Before joining Mattel, Kreiz was on the board and had a strong background in digital media. He sold Maker Studios to Disney in 2014, right after becoming CEO there.  

**Jamie:**  
And before that, he co-founded Fox Kids Europe. That was a huge kids’ entertainment network with channels in over 50 countries. Disney acquired it in 2002.  

**Raj:**  
He’s definitely got a track record in media and kids’ content. Kreiz is a protegé of Haim Saban, which probably helped shape his vision for Mattel.  

**Lena:**  
And just a quick note—Kreiz is originally from Israel and went to UCLA Anderson. He’s also on their board of advisers. That’s a solid academic and leadership background.  

**Alex:**  
So even though there have been some setbacks, the strategy is still in motion. It’ll be interesting to see how the movie and TV projects develop.

evidence_spans:
**Forms Plus – Frequently Asked Questions Report**

**Overview**  
At Forms Plus, we frequently receive inquiries regarding our services and capabilities. This report outlines some of the most commonly asked questions and provides detailed responses to enhance clarity and understanding of our offerings.

**1. Can Forms Plus create artwork for your product?**  
Yes, we can. Forms Plus is equipped with a team that possesses a keen eye for design and detail. We pride ourselves on being attentive listeners and can bring your vision to life with professional, industry-leading execution.

**2. Do we sell labels?**  
Yes, we offer a wide range of label types, including thermal transfer labels, direct thermal labels, laser label sheets, and integrated label sheets, among others.

**3. How does delivery work?**  
We provide a next-day delivery service for most products, although bespoke items may vary. We are committed to meeting your deadlines to ensure you are never short of stock. You can contact us for the best lead time and pricing.  
Additionally, you have the option to collect your products from our premises, which can help reduce delivery costs. This can be arranged through our ‘Shop Online’ portal.

**4. What are the minimum and maximum order quantities?**  
Our minimum order is one box, which contains either 1000 sheets of integrated labels or 500 sheets of laser labels. There is no maximum order limit—our goal is to accommodate all your needs, regardless of size.

**5. Can individuals without a registered business place an order?**  
Yes, they can. While VAT will be added to the purchase in such cases, a registered business is not a requirement to place an order with us.

**6. Are there other businesses similar to mine that use our forms?**  
Integrated labels are particularly popular among e-commerce businesses. We supply both local sole traders and well-known brands such as Boots, Boden, Joules, and Superdry, demonstrating our extensive industry experience.

**7. Can we process weekly or monthly orders?**  
Yes, this is a service that many of our customers appreciate. We can remind you to check your stock levels, and you can specify the quantity you require. We will then deliver the forms using our next-day delivery service.

**8. Who have we worked with?**  
Based in Cornwall, Forms Plus has supported numerous local businesses. Over our 35 years of experience, we have also worked with major brands including Superdry, Monsoon, Sainsbury’s, Boden, Lush, and White Stuff.

**Conclusion**  
Forms Plus is dedicated to providing tailored solutions to meet the needs of our diverse client base. Whether you are a small business or a large enterprise, we are equipped to deliver high-quality products and services with flexibility and professionalism.

Claim: Based in Seattle, Jones Root Beer has a unique tradition of changing the label art on a regular basis.
evidence_spans:
**Local Seattle Root Beer Stands the Test of Time**

A classic among root beer enthusiasts, Jones Root Beer has been a favorite for decades, with one fan recalling their first taste as part of a collection of just 10 root beers they ever tried. The fan discovered the drink at a gas station in southern Washington State while returning from a family trip to California, where they had also sampled the Mendocino Taubold’s Private Reserve Root Beer.

Based in Seattle, Jones Root Beer has a unique tradition of changing the label art on a regular basis. The images are submitted by fans and then voted on by the public. While the fan admits their own submission never made the cut, the company offers custom label options as a consolation for those who want a personal touch.

The root beer is described as sweet and pleasant, with a light but not weak body. It delivers a strong, fizzy carbonation that leans slightly on the harsh side, though not overpowering. The head is tall, though it dissipates a bit too quickly, the carbonation remains lively, keeping the drink enjoyable. The aftertaste is clean and true to the original flavor, fading gradually.

Overall, the fan finds nothing wrong with the drink and plenty to like. In fact, they revisited the root beer in 2005 to see if their initial enthusiasm had waned since first tasting it in 1998. Their opinion remained the same: they still enjoy it and consider it a solid choice. As they put it, they’ll “Run with the little guy” and continue to rate it among other root beers.

evidence_spans:

Greensburg, PA [ZIP Code]  

Dear Ms. Brisbane,  

I am writing to express my interest in the Incubator for the Arts program offered by the Westmoreland Cultural Trust. I recently learned about this initiative and was particularly inspired by the opportunity it provides for young and emerging artists to access affordable studio spaces and gain visibility within the community.  

The program, which began with the efforts of local artist Sean Daisher, known as “The Rev. Daisher Rocket,” has already demonstrated promising results. Mr. Daisher has been able to increase his visibility and sales since moving into the studio space at the Cultural Trust headquarters on North Main Street in Greensburg. His success, along with the experiences of others, has encouraged the trust to expand the initiative, making it a valuable resource for artists in the region.  

The Incubator for the Arts offers studio spaces for rent at a cost of $100 to $325 per month, which includes utilities and Internet access. This affordable option allows artists to work in a professional setting without the financial burden often associated with traditional retail spaces. As you mentioned, the trust is committed to supporting struggling artists by subsidizing some of the costs, recognizing that market value is not always feasible for those in the early stages of their careers.  

The program is expected to primarily serve college students and young artists, and I believe it aligns well with my current goals as an emerging artist. The opportunity to display artwork in the building’s windows, contribute to public murals, and participate in art exhibitions at the Palace Theatre would be invaluable in increasing my exposure and connecting with the local community.  

I would like to thank you and the Cultural Trust for your efforts in fostering a supportive environment for artists in Greensburg and Westmoreland County. It is clear that the arts scene is growing, and initiatives like the Incubator for the Arts are essential in nurturing that growth.  

I look forward to the possibility of becoming involved in this program and contributing to the vibrant cultural landscape of our community.  

Sincerely,  
[Your Name]

Claim: It didn’t rely on sight, but on sound—specifically, the unique noise signature of an approaching drone.
evidence_spans:
By 2015, the skies above the United States were expected to buzz with drones. As these flying machines became more common, it was wise for people to learn how to recognize the different types that might be passing overhead. Most would be the smaller, commercial drones—used for delivering Amazon packages, transporting medical supplies, conducting real-estate surveys, and even carrying mail. But for those living near the country’s borders, the view from above would be far more intense. These areas would see a steady presence of heavy-duty military drones, like the MQ-9 Reaper, built by General Atomics.

Yet, you didn’t always have to see a drone to know it was near. In Virginia, an inventor named Brian Hearing, Ph.D., had developed a new kind of drone-detection technology. It didn’t rely on sight, but on sound—specifically, the unique noise signature of an approaching drone. The idea had come to him after a strange and unexpected event. His business partner had lost control of a drone, which had flown into their neighbor’s backyard. As they watched from a distance, they were stunned by the clarity of the footage the drone captured. Inside the neighbor’s home, the family sat completely unaware, their every move recorded in high definition. It was this moment that sparked Hearing’s determination to find a way to detect these silent, watchful machines before they got too close.

Claim: Saudi officials considered announcing that Jamal Khashoggi died accidentally inside the consulate after news outlets reported that an interrogation had gone awry.
evidence_spans:
Saudi officials reportedly considered announcing that journalist Jamal Khashoggi died accidentally inside the Saudi consulate in Istanbul, where he had gone to obtain documents for a marriage license on October 2. The idea came after several news outlets reported late Monday that an interrogation had gone awry. According to The New York Times, Saudi Arabia had planned to release a report claiming Khashoggi’s death occurred two weeks ago.

A source told CNN that the report is expected to admit the interrogation occurred without proper authorization and that Saudi Arabia would take action against those responsible. Khashoggi, a Washington Post columnist, has not been seen since entering the consulate on October 2. Evidence suggests he was killed and dismembered inside the building.

Turkish officials reportedly revealed last week that they have audio and video evidence showing Khashoggi was interrogated, tortured, and then murdered. The New Yorker’s Robin Wright wrote last week that Khashoggi had feared for his life, saying, “Of course they’d like to see me out of the picture,” according to a conversation she had with him.

President Donald Trump commented Monday that “rogue killers” may be responsible for Khashoggi’s disappearance, adding that Saudi Arabia’s King Salman “firmly denied” any knowledge of the incident. While expressing concern over Khashoggi’s fate, Trump said he would not halt arms sales to the country.

Secretary of State Mike Pompeo made an urgent trip to Saudi Arabia on Monday to meet with King Salman.

Claim: Creating a 500-calorie daily deficit solely through exercise is not sustainable.
evidence_spans:
**Character A:** Have you ever heard that weight loss is mostly about diet?

**Character B:** I know people say that, but isn’t exercise just as important?

**Character A:** Actually, it’s the 80-20 rule—80% diet and 20% exercise. Without a calorie deficit, you won’t lose weight.

**Character B:** So you’re saying if I eat less than I burn, I’ll lose weight?

**Character A:** Exactly. Experts suggest a 500-calorie deficit per day to get the process going.

**Character B:** But can’t I just work out a lot to burn 500 calories?

**Character A:** You can, but it’s not sustainable. High-intensity workouts that burn that much are tough to keep up with long-term.

**Character B:** So what’s the better approach?

**Character A:** Cutting out high-calorie foods is easier and more effective. But you can’t rely on diet alone either.

**Character B:** Why not?

**Character A:** If you cut too much, you risk food deprivation and missing out on nutrients. You need balance.

**Character B:** I see. So you’re saying diet is the main driver, but you still need exercise?

**Character A:** Exactly. “Abs are made in the kitchen,” but they become visible with the right workouts.

**Character B:** So if I want to cut 600 calories a day, I should split it?

**Character A:** Yes—cut 300 through diet and burn 300 through exercise. It’s more balanced and sustainable.

**Character B:** And what about the benefits of working out?

**Character A:** It boosts metabolism, builds muscle, and tones your body—things diet alone won’t do.

**Character B:** So the key is combining both?

**Character A:** Definitely. A healthy diet and regular exercise lead to sustainable weight loss and a better physique.

**Character B:** I’ve heard the 80/20 rule before, but it makes more sense now.

**Character A:** That’s the takeaway—diet is more important, but exercise is still essential.

**Sarah:** Okay, so I just read this article about Judge David Miller changing his mind on the bond status for Adam Kaufman. You know, the Aventura guy accused of killing his wife.

**James:** Yeah, I saw that too. The article says he had an epiphany on Father’s Day and reconvened court this week to reconsider releasing Kaufman, even though he had previously denied it.

**Lena:** That’s actually pretty admirable, right? For a judge to re-evaluate a tough decision like that. It shows he’s not just sticking to his first ruling for the sake of it.

**James:** Definitely. It’s not easy to admit you might have made the wrong call, especially in a high-profile case. I think it shows real integrity.

**Sarah:** I agree. And it’s not like the prosecutors handled it gracefully. They threw a fit and filed a motion for Miller to recuse himself.

**Lena:** Classic. Like that’s ever going to work. You can’t just ask a judge to step down because you don’t like the ruling.

**James:** Right. It’s a tactic, but it’s not professional. Speaking of which, Bill Matthewman is representing the defense, right?

**Sarah:** Yep, that’s what we mentioned earlier.

**Lena:** Shifting gears a bit—did you know the deadline for US troops to withdraw from Iraq is this Tuesday?

**James:** No way. I haven’t heard much about that lately. It feels like it’s just disappeared from the news.

**Sarah:** Yeah, and now we’re all focused on Afghanistan. Speaking of that, I just picked up this book called *Horse Soldiers*. It’s about the CIA and special forces in Afghanistan right after 9/11.

**Lena:** Sounds interesting. I’ve heard it gives a good look into the region’s complex history and the challenges they faced.

**James:** Definitely worth a read, especially with the 4th of July coming up. Maybe we can all take a weekend to catch up on it.

**Sarah:** On that note, Florida is doing Operation Dry Water again. You know, the boating under the influence campaign.

**Lena:** Oxymoron much? Operation Dry Water? Like, water is already wet.

**James:** Haha, yeah, it’s kind of funny. But the message is clear: if you’re drinking, don’t drive or boat. Or really, do anything that requires coordination.

**Sarah:** True. But I say, if you’re just sitting on the couch, watching the Marlins and having a few drinks, that’s a solid weekend plan.

**Lena:** Second that. Let’s just hope the Marlins are actually worth watching.

Claim: Two cyclists have been killed in recent months on major cycling routes without proper bike lanes.
evidence_spans:
**Team Meeting Dialogue:**

**Alex:** Okay, let’s start with the latest incident. I think we all saw the news last night—another cyclist was hit and killed on Morgan Avenue near Meserole Street.  

**Jordan:** Yeah, that’s heartbreaking. A 30-year-old cyclist was killed. The truck driver turned right, clipped the rider, and didn’t stop. They found the truck a couple blocks away, but the cyclist was pronounced dead at the scene.  

**Maya:** I’m so sad to hear that. And the name hasn’t even been released yet. That’s got to be tough for the family.  

**Raj:** This is the second time in recent months. Remember Erica Abbott? She was killed on Bushwick Avenue in August. Both of these are major cycling routes without proper bike lanes.  

**Jordan:** Exactly. And both are in areas that are heavily used by cyclists going between Bushwick and Williamsburg. Morgan Avenue is mostly industrial—warehouses, food factories, chop shops. It’s a major truck route, even at night.  

**Maya:** So we’re dealing with a dangerous intersection of industrial traffic and cyclists who have nowhere safe to ride.  

**Alex:** It’s clear the need for a safe north-south bike lane is becoming more urgent. We need to push for infrastructure changes before another tragedy happens.  

**Raj:** Agreed. In the meantime, the police are still searching for the truck driver. I wonder if they’ve identified him yet.  

**Jordan:** They haven’t released any info on that. But I think the bigger issue is that we can’t keep waiting for something to happen before we act.  

**Maya:** Right. We need to make this a priority in our next outreach and planning meetings.  

**Alex:** Definitely. Let’s make sure we’re including this in our next report to the city and our community partners.

Claim: My goal was to capture some photos of the team, particularly David Wright and Manny Ramirez.
evidence_spans:
[Your Name]  
[Your Address]  
[City, State, ZIP Code]  
[Email Address]  
[Date]  

To: Mr. Pete [Last Name]  
     [Publication Name]  
     [Address]  
     [City, State, ZIP Code]  

Dear Mr. [Last Name],  

I hope this letter finds you well. I am writing to express my appreciation for your recent article, as well as to share a personal experience that underscores the growing importance of high-definition technology in both entertainment and professional settings.  

Last summer, while on a business trip to Atlanta, I attended a game between the Braves and the Boston Red Sox at Turner Field. My goal was to capture some photos of the team, particularly David Ortiz and Manny Ramirez. Unfortunately, my point-and-shoot Canon camera lacked the necessary zoom to do so. It was then that I realized I could capture clearer images by focusing on the Mitsubishi high-definition screen in centerfield—something that also appears on your publication’s cover. Not only did this provide a better vantage point, but it also allowed me to document the entire Red Sox lineup as they came to bat.  

This experience made me reflect on the transformative power of HD. High-definition is more than a visual enhancement—it is a shift in how we perceive and expect to experience content. As your article "One of These Things is Not Like the Others" suggests, clients are increasingly bringing their HD expectations from home into the workplace. They now demand similar quality in professional environments, often at consumer-level prices. While this can be a challenge for integrators, some are rising to meet the demand.  

At last month’s InfoComm, the topic of HD over the network—especially in video teleconferencing—was a focal point. Crestron president George Feldstein emphasized the need for dedicated AV networks to ensure sufficient bandwidth, a view shared by many in the industry. Similarly, Rick Snyder of Tandberg Americas noted that while most clients purchase systems with HD capabilities, only a minority actually utilize them.  

Clearly, HD is on the path to becoming a de facto standard. In the long term, future-proofing systems will be essential. However, in the short term, aligning client expectations with technological realities will remain a continuous challenge.  

We couldn’t agree more with your insights, Mr. [Last Name]. On behalf of myself and the readers of PRO AV, we are truly fortunate to have you contributing to our publication. Thank you for your thought-provoking work.  

Warm regards,  

[Your Full Name]  
[Your Title]  
[Your Company Name]

prompt:
Determine whether the claim is supported by the evidence.
Claim: Encrypted communications recovered from Wong's laptop detailed a planned shipment of 33 critically endangered Philippine forest turtles (Siebenrockiella leytensis) to a collector in Doha, facilitated by falsified CITES documentation from a non-existent Maldives-based wildlife export firm.
evidence_spans:
A man was arrested on Monday at Kuala Lumpur International Airport in Malaysia for attempting to smuggle endangered snakes. According to reports, the zipper on the suspect’s luggage malfunctioned, causing the bag to open and revealing a large number of reptiles. The haul included 95 endangered boa constrictors, as well as several non-endangered snakes and turtles. The arrest followed the discovery of the boas, as it is illegal to export endangered species without the appropriate permits.

The suspect, identified as Anson Wong, is not a first-time offender. National Geographic reports that Wong is widely regarded as a major figure in the global wildlife smuggling trade. Bryan Christy of National Geographic notes that Wong, based in Penang, runs one of the world’s largest legal reptile supply companies, which has previously been used as a cover for smuggling critically endangered wildlife from countries including Australia, China, Madagascar, New Zealand, and South America. His illicit trade has reportedly included items such as snow leopard pelts, panda bear skins, rhino horns, rare birds, and Komodo dragons.

Wong was previously arrested in 1990 as part of a five-year sting operation conducted by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. He served prison time until his release in 2003. His latest arrest has resulted in a six-month prison sentence and a fine of $61,000. Authorities also seized Wong’s laptop and cell phone, which may provide valuable information to help dismantle other smuggling operations.

Wildlife smuggling remains a pervasive issue due to the relatively low risk of detection and prosecution. Christy explains that wildlife trafficking is among the most profitable forms of transnational organized crime, not only due to high profit margins but also because of the minimal likelihood of being caught. Law enforcement agencies worldwide are often underfunded and understaffed in their efforts to combat the trade. Even when smugglers are apprehended, penalties are typically limited to fines that are often as minor as a parking ticket.

Christy views Wong’s recent arrest as a positive development, suggesting that law enforcement agencies are beginning to make progress in addressing the global wildlife smuggling trade.

prompt:
Determine whether the claim is supported by the evidence.
Claim: The distance from the pitcher's mound to home plate in professional baseball is 60 feet and 6 inches.
evidence_spans:
**Interviewer:** Thanks for joining us today. For those who might be new to SkillShow, can you explain what kind of information people can find on your FAQ page?

**Expert:** Absolutely. Whether you're new to SkillShow or a long-time user, our FAQ page is a great resource for the most common questions. If you can’t find what you’re looking for there, we encourage you to send us an email and someone will be in touch quickly.

**Interviewer:** What can people expect in a SkillShow Premium Video?

**Expert:** Premium Videos typically include a player introduction, multiple camera angles of your primary defensive skills, multiple angles of batting practice, and your 60-yard dash. It’s designed to give a comprehensive look at a player’s abilities.

**Interviewer:** How do customers receive their videos?

**Expert:** After the video is edited, you’ll receive a digital copy via email that you can download for your own use. For Perfect Game players, the video will also be posted on their PG Profile Page for easy access and sharing.

**Interviewer:** How long does the editing process usually take?

**Expert:** Our standard editing time is about four weeks, but we also offer a one-week rush edit option for those who need it sooner.

**Interviewer:** Can you film a player during an actual game?

**Expert:** No, we don’t recommend filming during games for a quality skills video. There are too many variables, and it’s difficult to capture consistent performance. Coaches prefer a ‘pro-style’ workout for evaluation. That said, we do film pitchers during games, but only in between innings.

**Interviewer:** What if a player isn’t happy with their video?

**Expert:** We want to make sure you’re completely satisfied. You’ll have up to one week to review your video and request any changes if needed.

**Interviewer:** Why is having a video so important for a player’s recruitment?

**Expert:** A coach needs to see your skills to determine if you can play at their level. A resume alone doesn’t allow them to evaluate your projectability as an athlete. Your video is your resume—it’s your first and often only chance to make a strong impression. It’s the primary way many players get in front of the schools they’re interested in.

**Interviewer:** When is the best time to start creating a video?

**Expert:** You can never start too early. College coaches are always looking ahead. The earlier you get on their radar, the better. In the past, recruiting often began in a player’s senior year, but now verbal commitments are being offered to sophomores and even freshmen.

**Interviewer:** How often should a player update their video?

**Expert:** At least once a year, or any time you feel like your game has changed. That could be when you’ve gotten bigger, stronger, faster, or when you’ve improved your mechanics since your last video.

**Interviewer:** Is there a specific time of year when coaches are less interested in recruiting?

**Expert:** That’s a common misconception. Coaches are in recruiting mode all year long. College teams are constantly turning over players due to graduation, injury, the draft, or lack of playing time. A coach needs a database of potential recruits to keep their team strong each year. A video is a great tool to get on a coach’s radar and make it easy for them to follow your progress. In fact, most coaches prefer to see a video when a player reaches out.

**Expert:** At least once a year, or any time you feel like your game has changed. That could be when you’ve gotten bigger, stronger, faster, or when you’ve improved your mechanics since your last video.

**Interviewer:** Is there a specific time of year when coaches are less interested in recruiting?

**Expert:** That’s a common misconception. Coaches are in recruiting mode all year long. College teams are constantly turning over players due to graduation, injury, the draft, or lack of playing time. A coach needs a database of potential recruits to keep their team strong each year. A video is a great tool to get on a coach’s radar and make it easy for them to follow your progress. In fact, most coaches prefer to see a video when a player reaches out.
